picks.headline: Best value Montana creek land under $150k
picks.reason: At $8,128/acre for 18.3 acres with year-round creek water, this is the strongest value proposition in the set by a wide margin. Timber and hunting add real self-sufficiency potential. Seasonal access is a genuine concern that needs verification before committing, but no red flags and solid water is a rare combination at this price.

picks.headline: Existing cabin with creek and timber rights
picks.reason: The existing cabin on 11.5 acres is a significant practical advantage — avoiding a full build-out lowers the effective entry cost despite the $47,826/acre price tag. Water rights and nearby creek support off-grid viability. Year-round road access and utility details must be confirmed, but no red flags and a livable structure make this immediately actionable.

picks.headline: National Forest border with exceptional water access
picks.reason: Surrounded by National Forest on a 5-acre parcel gives de facto access to much larger wild land while keeping ownership costs bounded. The listing notes creeks, rivers, and a nearby lake — unusually strong water access — plus an existing shop structure. At $44,970/acre it is expensive for the acreage, and well/septic installs are required costs to budget for.

picks.headline: Massive ranch with water, wrong scale for one family
picks.reason: Nearly 30,000 acres with creek water and grazing infrastructure at $401/acre is extraordinary land economics, but the $12M price makes it commercial territory, not a homestead buy. For a group or cooperative with real capital, the self-sufficiency potential (water rights, timber, hunting, grazing) is unmatched in this set. Ranked 4th because the scale disqualifies it for most individual homesteaders.

picks.headline: Mostly leased land, seasonal-only, not homestead viable
picks.reason: Only 3,710 of 35,000 acres are actually deeded — the rest is BLM and State lease that cannot support permanent residence or development. Seasonal-use designation and deed restrictions make year-round self-sufficient living impossible here. It ranks last because the core homesteading requirements (buildability, year-round residence, true land ownership) are fundamentally unmet.
